transcript.text: Hey, happy Friday, Jay.
Hey, Tyler, how are you doing?
This meeting is being recorded.
Doing pretty well.
Got to travel to our office in San Francisco this week and get out of Houston for a little bit.
So that's always nice.
Awesome.
Cool.
Nice.
Where are you based?
I am based in Salt Lake City in Utah, but originally from the Philadelphia area and lived in the Bay Area and Texas also.
So kind of all over.
Okay, nice.
Were you just in Texas during like the NBA phase of your life or did you stay there for a little while?
yeah, for a couple of years.
Exactly, exactly.
Nice.
Yeah, Austin's a good place if you like the outdoors and are going to live in Utah.
You probably don't want to go to Houston in terms of the hiking options there.
Yeah, good food, good stuff in Houston, but yeah, it's definitely a different world for sure.
Well, great to meet you.
you.
Thank Thank

For context on my side, I'm the founding AE here at GrowthX, so I work hand-in-hand with our CEO, Marcel, and really just looking to use this call to understand how you found us, what you're looking for in a potential partner, and give you the breakdown on everything we do so that you can sort of understand for the right fit.
That would be great.
Yeah, I'd love to.
I'm trying to, I mean, just candidly, like, stay up to date on the different AI or agent-like tools that could potentially help marketing, either from outreach or an operational perspective.

So, yeah, exciting to understand exactly what you do and how it's different from maybe some other tools out there, like Unify or Clay, and I know there's probably some overlap, but it'd be great to understand what you guys, specifically, like, what's the most important use case that you guys really have?
Yeah, 100%.
I mean, high level, we partner with our clients as, like, an extension of their content team and pretty much go strategy to analyze.
Analytics on anything related to content for AI search, traditional search, so SEO.
How did you come across us?
Like, where did you find us?
Because unifying play are typically not, like, competitors that people are evaluating against us.
Yeah, I don't know.
It could have been LinkedIn.
I can't recall exactly.
It might have been a post on LinkedIn, most likely, is probably where I read about you guys.
Okay, nice, nice.
And I guess, like, what would you say the specifics are that you're looking to solve for?
Is it, like, AI search visibility?
Is it more specific than that?
Well, some of the challenge, and I think what caught my eye with GrowthX, and maybe I'm off here, but just us bringing together, like, reporting from different sources.
Like, we recently migrated over to HubSpot, which has been great.
But because we're an enterprise, or a company selling into large enterprises, we're
Because it can be hard for us to continually track, hey, where did this lead hear about us?
Did they play a role in the opportunity?
kind of tracking some of that customer journey work that's important to understanding how people are learning about our product.
And the analytics is getting those tracked and reported on requires still a lot of manual work.
So I was curious to see if there was something that this product could do to help us out with that.
Gotcha, gotcha.
I hear what you're saying.
No, we're typically working more as like an agency partner, but we are developing some products.
And most customers, I would say, are working with us because they need a way to scale content creation.
And they don't want to start from zero building workflows in a tool like AirOps.
They don't want to maybe like build out a large team.
Okay.
And so...
So as like a quick example, you know, Augment Code, they compete against Cursor, they compete against GitHub, they're in more of like the AI coding space.
Oh, gotcha, gotcha.
We created all of these guides for them, right?
So they're pretty technical.
They're definitely like catered to a developer or like a CTO at an enterprise.
But, you know, people partner with us because they don't have like the resources or the technology to create this kind of optimized content internally.
It sounds like you need more something to help with attribution.
this is a piece too.
Maybe, you know, I just misremembered, like our content creation engine is challenging, right?
Like we've got, you know, we can pay folks to write stuff, but historically we found that that is really challenging for a new market.
And we end up having to write a lot of the stuff or rewrite a lot of the stuff ourselves.
We don't have an internal content, right?
Or we heavily utilize check GPT or perplexity.
It helped us brainstorm and write things, but it's definitely having a consistent flow of content is a challenge for us.
Okay, nice.
So like the content you're writing today, it is done in-house, or you guys use like an agency partner and freelancers to do that today?
Both, mostly freelancers.
And then we work internally, myself and a director of demand gen, using AI tools to write stuff, trying to bring in our, you know, VP of product or CTO to help write things as well.
We also have a webinar program that we run, and it's a lot.
It's a lot to keep up with.
And I think we kind of go in ebbs and flows with content.
We're probably doing too much.
I'm like, what was this AI governance framework piece for, which is for augment code?
Yeah, so I would say the content we're taking...
typically creating is editorial in nature, maybe programmatic.
We've got a slide and I'll tell you a little bit about like our origin story.
But this gives you like a look at the specifics in terms of the content we typically are creating.
Comparisons, alternatives, lead magnets, even like use case pages.
And so in our approach, we have two phases.
The first we call a strategy sprint, where we're really like developing the context engineering or the workflows that we use to create content.
There's also like experts in the loop for each step of the process.
And so what we're doing is we're starting and making sure we get the quality right, the roadmap of assignments we would create.
And then in phase two, we aim to publish five articles every week or refresh existing content.
And so a lot of times, like our partners, you know, take augment code.
The first thing that they want to do is drive more AI visibility, but they ultimately want...
Higher intent traffic conversions on the site.
I'll look at like what we did through the guides we created for them.
When they started with us, they were around like 20K monthly organic.
They were spending quite a bit on paid.
Fast forward, like just six months, we've created over 100 guides for them.
They've 3X their organic traffic.
They're starting to see our content get cited and course searches for them where, you know, they want their content to be used as citations for other, you know, options that you see here.
So I think, you know, the key is, right, like, do you have more of a challenge on like analytics and how you're reporting?
We've got both.
Like there's both.
And so, I mean, just being candid, I can't exactly remember.
I it's been a little bit since I actually saw GrowthX.
I wasn't entirely sure what it did.
So I was kind of like talking about what was top of mind.
But our content piece is a challenge.
So I'm certainly interested in understanding more about what you can do because I'm trying to figure out how to do that at scale.
Yeah, definitely.
I guess what have you thought about in terms of like buying tools to use AI as part of content creation or like building a team in-house?
Like what are you thinking are the best options right now?
Yeah.
So outsourcing, number one, like working with somebody who can write for us on LinkedIn.

And, you know, in kind of ghostwriting for our CEO and CTO, and then working with kind of a journalist or paid agents who can go in and help write regular articles for us on specific, you know, either thought leadership topics or technical topics.
And then thirdly, like in-house, like pulling from our own experience with like our customer success team and our partnership teams, right?
Like specific articles and leveraging tools like chat GPT to help us.
So, yeah.
We also work with companies that help us create lead magnets, like we do an industry report every year, so that's outsourced as well.
So it's a combination of outsourced, in-house, and AI is how I'm doing it today.
Okay, nice.
And I guess what challenges have you run into with leveraging AI?

Because I feel like that's typically where people will choose us, is they just haven't been able to either build something that tailors the content enough, or they get stuck trying to figure out how to orchestrate the different workflows for where they would do the deep research, right?
Like how they would actually create context that feeds into the workflows.
So how far along have you guys gotten with leveraging the AI specifically?
I mean, pretty far.
We use it kind of for everything.
It's not by itself, right?
Like I don't trust it to go out and write me.
hopefully we you.
A, you know, a full-length blog article and just post it.
needs to be kind of like a brainstorming piece and we can use it for cleaning up something that's written by a human and iterating that way.
But there's nothing that we're doing where we trust it to just write something that's going to be useful for our audience just because the market that we're in is new.
And a lot of the times, if you've got, you get a lot of inaccuracies or it's just too bland to be useful from a SEO, geo, or thought leadership perspective.
That makes sense.
Yeah.
And I think I'll show you a little bit about like how the workflows are leveraged to create content.
But one piece just on GrowthX is like one differentiator for us is like we built this company from our founder and CEO who had built content engines at every stop in his career.
And so like, you know, he's never spent...
Much unpaid.
At IBM, he built their first microsite that lived outside of the website for over a decade, drove $17 million in revenue for them in the first year.
And then at some of these high-growth companies, he joined early as the marketing leader.
And basically, what he did at DeepGram was he replicated this process of building AI workflows where you could automate.
And when he was at DeepGram, he just saw that they could get to quality much faster.
They could drive traffic a lot faster than building a team of writers.
And so Marcel started hosting these workshops, showing people how he put the workflows together.

And then eventually, was like enough people wanted him to build it for them that he started GrowthX as this agency where we've got our internal platform that our forward-deployed team is using to create content.
On behalf of our customers, but what we're doing here is we're setting up these pipelines.
Where we go super deep and have agentic workflows set up for things like the research so that if it's not at a certain quality score, it's going to rerun that research and address gaps.
And what our team is doing is they're basically helping control all of the inputs that we use so that, you know, take Lovable, we've created all these guides for Lovable.
We're also, like, using their products to programmatically create these different app templates for different use cases.
And so, like, everything that you're seeing here, you know, our team created, including the web development and design.
But what we're doing is we're actually going super deep into, like, who is this being written for?
How should it be structured?
How should our research be set up?
And, like, what places should we not research from?
And then, you know, it's just a problem.
Here, what am I looking at?
Yeah, so this is the inputs for this first step in our workflow, which is the research step.
But if you actually inspect the workflow, what you'll see is that there are several steps right in between here.
We're generating this research plan.
We're going out and we're synthesizing the results from all the research that we've done.
There's like a bibliography of sources that are used.

And what you can see from like the workflow catalog for this workflow in particular here is that within those steps, you know, there are large prompts that we've crafted for generating a research plan for synthesizing the results.
And then what we do is we use that in tandem with the artifacts that we've created around their personas, around their company, around their brand guidelines.
So I wanted to show you that there's like.
Something running in the background right now where I can't show you the artifacts in particular, but that's what's actually guiding these workflows is the prompts and then the artifacts.
And then this is just like the output from each step of the process.
Interesting.
Really interesting.
So what we do, right, is we ingest resources from your team.
We set up like a product deep dive so that we can get into the nuances of the platform.
And then we set up this roadmap of assignments where everything is connected to search volume, competitor content, the keyword difficulty of actually ranking and showing up.
And then we create like this massive roadmap of different assignments that we can create for you around different topics and start moving through and basically creating drafts that your team reviews.
But that's the extent of what you need to do, right?
So you're getting like the five articles per week.

You're getting the quality that you're trying to hit using tools internally, but you're getting it faster and more consistently because we've taken like 70 plus clients through this process that is very tailored to like technical B2B software customers.
And so I think as the next step, like what I can do is send you a lot of examples of content we've created.
I can give you some different case studies of like the results we got to.
And then I think, you know, next time I'm logging in, I can like actually get into the weeds a little bit more and, you know, show you how like the content gets created.
But I'm also happy to answer any other questions that you have just on this intro call.
Yeah, so just for clarity, you're like a hybrid platform and agency, so offer both software and service here.
Which is great.
Who is doing the writing and the prompting in here to help make this whole engine go?
Is it me?
Is it somebody from my team?
Or is that part of the service that you get?
Yeah, that's part of the service.
So it's definitely like a model where the team that you're working with, you know, we basically have a bunch of writers, SEO experts, AI engineers, and they're setting up these pipelines.
They're creating the artifacts or the inputs.
They're creating the roadmap based on the analysis that they're doing around what the actual search opportunity is.
And we literally just need you to help us calibrate on the quality of our articles and then review the drafts once we get into more of a steady state.
So the vision is definitely to...
Let your team have access to be able to start to modify these pipelines themselves.
We just have the platform as something that we use internally today.
And what our customers have access to is a product that's more like Profound that's on the AI visibility and monitoring side.
And so we'll set that up for you.
You don't have to worry about setting up the right prompts to track.
But that's the only thing you log into today.
In the future, we would be moving to let our customers actually modify these pipelines or create content without our help.
But right now, it's all done by our team.
Okay.
And then content quality score, visibility score.
Is AI visibility score, is that, I mean, it's a percentage.
Like, is that, hey, you're doing really well on chat GPT or perplexity or co-pilot?
Like, that, I don't know if 84% is good, if it's bad.
Like, what does it mean?
And I...
How much are you guys focused on helping to build that strategy on the generative engine and the SEO side?
Yeah, definitely.

So this is like something that we're still kind of working on internally, but the vision for a visibility score would be of all the prompts that you're tracking, what percentage are you showing up against your competitors?

And so, you know, we use a partner scrunch for that as we're putting the finishing touches on our product, and this would show you your visibility against all the prompts that you're tracking against competitors who are also, you know, showing up in those prompts.

And then with the AI search and the SEO strategy, like, we're absolutely helping take the lead on, here's all the different opportunities that we see, here's where you should focus, like, your content creation efforts, and then we're building the reporting so that you can...
And see like how that's improving on a week-by-week basis.
Okay.
And what's your pricing model?
Yeah, how we set up our pricing.
So in phase one, it's a 40K fixed fee.
And we will start publishing typically by week four.
So you'll probably have about five to 10 articles by the end of the strategy sprint that we'll publish in your CMS.
And then in phase two, again, we're doing five articles per week.
We will forward deploy like AI engineers to customize the pipeline for you or maybe tackle a use case that's a bit different, like hosting on LinkedIn instead of the marketing site.
And then we charge 18K per month on phase two.
Gotcha.
Eight weeks and then 12.
And what's the commitment?
Is it a full year commitment?
Commitment is initially just...
Phase 1, and you have the ability to like opt in to Phase 2 by the end of the engagement.
So you do have to make a decision by the end of Phase 1, but you don't have to necessarily like commit to the 12-month Phase 2 until the end of Phase 1.

And how many pieces of content are you delivering?
And maybe that's the wrong way to think about it.

Is that a LinkedIn post?
I guess I'm.

And how many should I expect, you know, across a quarter?
Yeah, for sure.
We definitely target five, either net new or refreshed per week.
And if you wanted to take a strategy, that was more, okay, we're going for technical depth here, right?
We're going for longer pieces.
We're going for like thought leadership.
Then we would go less in terms of content per week, because like what you're looking for, it's just going to require like longer review cycles.
But, you know, when it comes to like the articles that we publish, typically everything is on the website.
And if we were doing something that was more social, it would be because there's like a really important need behind it, or that there's less of a need on like the website specifically.
So I think, you know, like sometimes like the client might be able
A little too niche for like the scale that our partnership gives benefits for.

Like you have to have enough content that would be worthwhile to create.
Yeah, totally.

That's right.

Yeah.
I mean, it is expensive like for us, right?

that a typical target for you guys and somebody or are you guys like, I mean, I saw Airbnb, right?

think let me send you some more technical pieces that we've created just so you can look at the articles and sort of connect like is that what we need?
